coredata.dc:title: Customized multi-stimuli nanovehicles with dissociable ‘bomblets’ for photothermal-enhanced synergetic tumor therapy

coredata.dc:description: Recently, the therapeutic effect of chemotherapy has been obviously impaired due to premature drug release, low tumor penetration, and multidrug resistance of nanoplatforms. In this paper, a novel multiple-sensitive drug delivery system (MC-ss-CDs) was developed by gating long-wavelength emitting carbon dots (CDs) on the openings of mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MC) through disulfide bonds. The MC with excellent photothermal transition efficiency and high drug storage capacity for doxorubicin (DOX) was used as the delivery carrier. The CDs had multiple functions, including intelligent switching to hinder unwanted release, photothermal therapy (PTT) agents to improve the heat generation effect of MCs and bioimaging trackers to monitor drug delivery. The disulfide bonds, as the linkers between MC carriers and CDs, are stable under normal physical conditions and relatively labile under high GSH concentrations in the cytoplasm of tumor cells. After arriving at the tumor microenvironment, DOX/MC-ss-CDs can rapidly break into DOX/MC and CDs under high GSH concentrations. DOX/MC could realize efficient integration of PTT and chemotherapy on the surface of the tumor by stimuli-responsive DOX release and synergetic heating of MC and CDs. The small-sized CDs with excellent penetrating ability could effectively enter the deep tumor and realize NIR-triggered photothermal ablation. The DOX/MC-ss-CDs showed a chemophotothermal effect with a combination index of 0.38 in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, the DOX/MC-ss-CDs could be employed as a trackable nanovehicle for synergistic chemotherapy and PTT at different depths.

TEM images of (A) MC-COOH, (B) MC-ss-CDs and (C) MC-ss-CDs after incubation with 10 mM GSH; (D) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and (E) pore size distribution curves based on adsorption branch of MC-COOH, MC-ss-COOH and MC-ss-CDs; (F) Corresponding Zeta potential values after each modification; and (G) particle size distributions and PDI of MC-COOH, MC-ss-COOH and MC-ss-CDs; (G) Images of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS and water; (H) HSA adsorbance percentage of MC-COOH, MC-ss-COOH and MC-ss-CDs; (I) Hemolysis percentages of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs with different concentrations (n = 3).

(A) NIR-triggered temperature elevation of different concentrations of MC-ss-CDs at 1 W/cm2; The photostability of 100 μg/mL (B) MC-COOH and (C) MC-ss-CDs over 4 cycles; Linear time data versus –lnθ from the cooling curve of (D) MC-COOH and (E) MC-ss-CDs photothermal effect curves (illustrations); (F) XRD patterns of DOX, physical mixture, MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-ss-CDs; (G) In vitro drug release of DOX/MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-COOH at pH 7.4 and pH 5.5 PBS; (H) Drug release of DOX/MC-COOH and DOX/MC-ss-CDs in presence of GSH or not; (I) Drug release profiles of DOX/MC-ss-CDs in presence of 10 mM GSH and NIR irradiation at 2.0 W/cm2 at pH 5.5 PBS, respectively (n = 3).

(Bar = 50 µm).

(A) CLSM and (B) the fluorescence intensity of 4T1 cells incubated with different preparations at a DOX dose of 10 μg/mL (Bar = 50 µm); (C) The optical images of 4T1 MTSs treated with various samples (Bar = 100 µm); The cell viabilities of 4T1 cells (D) without NIR laser irradiation and (E) under the NIR laser irradiation (n = 5); (F) Tumor penetration of MC-ss-CDs in vivo with CDs as fluorescent tracker (scale bar=100 µm); (G) Fluorescence imaging of isolated-main organs after administration of DOX/MC-ss-CDs and free DOX at 2, 6 and 12 h; (H) Fluorescence semi-quantification of DOX intensity at 2, 6 and 12 h (n = 3);.

(A-E) Tumor growth curves and the representative tumor pictures of mice; (F) Body weight change curves of mice; (G) Relative tumor mass; (H) Thermal imaging photographs of mice after administrated with saline and MC-ss-CDs at 1 W/cm2 NIR irradiation. (n = 5, ***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01 and *p < 0.05). (I) H&E staining of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney from different treatment groups; (J) Biochemical indicators after treatment of Saline and MC-ss-CDs + NIR.

head.ce:title: Customized multi-stimuli nanovehicles with dissociable ‘bomblets’ for photothermal-enhanced synergetic tumor therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the therapeutic effect of chemotherapy has been obviously impaired due to premature drug release, low tumor penetration, and multidrug resistance of nanoplatforms. In this paper, a novel multiple-sensitive drug delivery system (MC-ss-CDs) was developed by gating long-wavelength emitting carbon dots (CDs) on the openings of mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MC) through disulfide bonds. The MC with excellent photothermal transition efficiency and high drug storage capacity for doxorubicin (DOX) was used as the delivery carrier. The CDs had multiple functions, including intelligent switching to hinder unwanted release, photothermal therapy (PTT) agents to improve the heat generation effect of MCs and bioimaging trackers to monitor drug delivery. The disulfide bonds, as the linkers between MC carriers and CDs, are stable under normal physical conditions and relatively labile under high GSH concentrations in the cytoplasm of tumor cells. After arriving at the tumor microenvironment, DOX/MC-ss-CDs can rapidly break into DOX/MC and CDs under high GSH concentrations. DOX/MC could realize efficient integration of PTT and chemotherapy on the surface of the tumor by stimuli-responsive DOX release and synergetic heating of MC and CDs. The small-sized CDs with excellent penetrating ability could effectively enter the deep tumor and realize NIR-triggered photothermal ablation. The DOX/MC-ss-CDs showed a chemophotothermal effect with a combination index of 0.38 in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, the DOX/MC-ss-CDs could be employed as a trackable nanovehicle for synergistic chemotherapy and PTT at different depths.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, tumors have become the main threat to human health, and correspondingly, various powerful means have been developed to treat tumors, including surgical resection, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Among them, chemotherapy has become an up-and-coming approach in cancer treatment. However, there are still some shortcomings in this conventional method, such as the severe toxicity of anticancer drugs and poor efficacy of individual chemotherapies because of the lack of tumor-specific targeting ability and multidrug resistance [1,2].

Photothermal therapy (PTT), a noninvasive and efficacious therapy with few systemic side effects, has attracted tremendous attention in cancer treatment. Because of the deeper penetration ability and less harm to the human body [3], near-infrared (NIR) light (700–1200 nm) can be absorbed by nanocomposites and converted into cytotoxic heat in tumor tissues [4]. The resultant hyperthermia can accelerate anticancer drug release from carriers, facilitate nanocomposite uptake by tumor cells and make cancer cells susceptible to chemotherapeutics [5,6]. Accordingly, the therapeutic efficacy of chemo-PTT can be superior to that of a single treatment.

With the development of hyperthermia, various PTT adsorbing agents have been developed, such as precious metal materials (Au and Pt) [7,8], metal compounds (CuS, ZnS and MOF materials) [9] and carbon materials (graphene and carbon nanotubes [10–14]). Among these PTT adsorbing agents, mesoporous carbon (MC) nanoparticles have attracted considerable attention because of their extensive satisfactory properties, including porosity, large specific surface area, tunable pore size, easily functionalized surface, and good biocompatibility. In addition to the ones mentioned above, the unique supramolecular π-π stacking for loading drugs [15,16] and photothermal conversion efficiency make MCs extraordinary nanocarriers for thermochemotherapy.

Nevertheless, the application of chemo-PTT drug delivery systems (DDSs) based on MC is still unsatisfactory. On the one hand, the premature release of drugs before the targeted sites could also cause severe toxicity and adverse effects on normal cells, leading to an undesirable outcome. Therefore, the development of stimuli-responsive DDSs, which hold accurate drug release based on nanomaterials, has received increasing achievements through convenient switching effects induced by specific stimulations [17–19] such as lysosomal pH [20], high intracellular glutathione (GSH) concentration [21], enzymes [22], light [23], heat [24], magnetism [25] and so on. For instance, the cleavable disulfide bonds, which are used to fix the gatekeepers on the nanocarrier surface to prevent the early leakage of drug, could be broken by the high GSH concentration, triggering the release of the drug. On the other hand, the restricted tumor penetration ability, which is caused by the dense extracellular matrix of solid tumors, enhanced interstitial fluid pressure, and disorganized vasculature, is a severe hindrance in targeted delivery systems [26–28]. A smaller particle size has a stronger diffusion ability [29,30], and small NPs are beneficial to tumor penetration. Under such circumstances, size-changeable nanoparticles have been developed, which have relatively larger initial particle sizes for longer circulation times and fast disassembly into small particles for enhanced intratumoral diffusion [31,32], and the initial larger size could prevent the small nanoparticles from being cleared out of the blood circulation rapidly as well [32,33]. Thus, small NPs with an inspired photothermal effect can be modified on the MC surface to impede unwanted drug release; the resultant dissociated NPs can forward penetrate the tumor depth to generate heat to achieve efficient chemo-PTT combination therapy in the deep and superficial sites of the tumor.

Currently, carbon dots (CDs), as novel fluorescent nanoparticles, have attracted exceptional attention owing to their considerable advantages, including their high photostability, water dispersibility, satisfactory biocompatibility and facile preparation and modification [34]. Most importantly, their good luminescence peculiarity is advantageous for bioimaging, thus facilitating monitoring [35]. However, most existing CDs emit blue/green fluorescence with a relatively weak imaging penetration capacity [36,37]. According to a previous report, short wavelength light has higher energy with poor penetration capability. Hence, long wavelength emitting CDs with excellent photothermal conversion capacity are highly needed to exploit PTT in deeper tumors [38]. Therefore, combining MC and CDs can also achieve in vivo real-time imaging since CDs exhibit multicolor and wavelength-dependent fluorescence upon laser excitation [39]. In addition, the released CDs with diameters between 2 nm and 10 nm could certainly have a higher penetration capability to produce a PTT effect inside the tumor tissues.

In this work, a redox/NIR/pH triple-responsive nanoplatform (MC-ss-CDs) based on CDs-gated MC was developed for PTT-enhanced and tumor-penetrated thermochemotherapy, as shown in Scheme 1. The small fluorescent CDs with excellent photothermal capacity and tumor penetration ability were modified on the openings of MC through disulfide bonds to hinder the untimely release of chemotherapeutic drugs and enhance the PTT therapeutic effect. The broad-spectrum antitumor agent doxorubicin (DOX) was chosen as a model drug. Once the DOX-loaded DOX/MC-ss-CDs nanoplatforms were delivered to the tumor sites and internalized into tumor cells, the high concentration GSH cleaved the disulfide bonds, resulting in redox-responsive drug release inside the tumor sites. The detached CDs could enter deep-seated tumor tissue to further generate cytotoxic heat and enhance the PTT effect under NIR irradiation, which could promote the release of the drug and increase the tumor sensitivity to the chemotherapeutics. Furthermore, the recovery of CDs fluorescence is significant in the visualization and imaging of the tumor, which could provide an accurate position and state of the nanoplatform. In conclusion, the facile and versatile DOX/MC-ss-CDs nanoplatforms can obtain an excellent loading efficiency, intracellular-triggered drug release and PTT-enhanced synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy, improve tumor penetration and track fluorescent probes, which may be an encouraging nanoplatform for tumor treatment.

Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 2,2'-Dipyridyl disulfide, cysteamine hydrochloride, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), glutathione (GSH), DOX and were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China). Cell culture media RPMI-1640, penicillin-streptomycin and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were supplied by GIBCO, Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, USA). All analytical reagents were not further purified before use.

CDs were prepared using a simple and green method. In brief, 6.0 g urea and 4.0 g citric acid were added to 30 mL DMF. The above mixture was settled in an autoclave and placed at 190 °C for 8 h. Then, the produced sample was purified by dialyzing against ultrapure water and freeze-dried. The MC NPs were prepared based on reported work with some modifications [40]. S-(2-aminoethylthio)− 2-thiopyridine hydrochloride (Py-ss-NH2) and oxidized MC (MC-COOH) were prepared following the reported work [41]. MC-COOH (30 mg) was added to 15 mL pH 5 PBS by ultrasound, and NHS and EDC were added to activate carboxylic groups followed by the addition of 20 mg Py-ss-NH2 to prepare MC-ss-Py. Subsequently, 3-mercaptopropionic acid (100 μL) was mixed with MC-ss-Py solution under gentle stirring for 24 h to prepare MC-ss-COOH. After that, the MC-ss-COOH was further activated to graft CDs and reacted overnight. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged to collect MC-ss-CDs.

DOX was applied to evaluate the responsive release property and photothermal- chemotherapy of DOX/MC-ss-CDs. The solvent adsorption method was applied to load DOX before the capping of CDS. MC-ss-COOH (20 mg) was added in PBS (pH 7.4), followed by addition of 5 mL DOX (3 mg/mL). And the mixture was continuously stirred for 18 h. Then, the mixture was centrifuged and washed. Afterwards, the DOX-loaded MC-ss-COOH was activated with EDC and NHS in pH 7.4 PBS for 1 h, and then CDs were added in the suspension to capping the opening of MC to prepare DOX-loaded MC-ss-CDs (DOX/MC-ss-CDs). Meanwhile, the eluent of loading process was at gathered every step to quantify the loading capacity using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 481 nm.

The loading efficiency (LE%) was calculated by the following formula.
LE% = mDOX/(mDOX + mC) ×100
where mDOX is the mass of DOX entrapped in carriers, and mC is the total mass of carriers added.

UVvis spectrophotometry was employed to determine the NIR/pH/redox triple-responsive release properties of DOX at 481 nm. The DOX release properties from DOX/MC-ss-CDs were measured in different PBS media at pH 5.5, pH 7.4, pH 5.5 with 10 mM GSH, and pH 7.4 with 10 mM GSH. The as-prepared DOX/MC-ss-CDs samples were dispersed in release media shaking at 37 °C. At designated intervals, 1 mL of the release medium was withdrawn to determine the absorbance and then added back to the medium. The samples of irradiation groups were irradiated by an 808 nm laser at 2 W/cm2 for 5 min at the determined time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal effect evaluation and stability
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of samples with serials of concentrations were irradiated by an 808 nm laser to measure the power-dependent and concentration-dependent photothermal effects. The system temperature was recorded by a thermal infrared imager (InfraTec GmbH, Infrarotsensorik und Messtechnik, Germany). Distilled water was used as the control group. The photothermal stability of samples was evaluated by irradiating and cooling the samples. The detailed calculation process of photothermal conversion efficiency (η) is supplied in the Supporting information.

4T1 cells were incubated in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10 % FBS and 5 % CO2. After the cells were seeded in 6-well plates for 24 h, different concentrations of DOX/MC-ss-CDs were added and coincubated with the cells for 2 h. Then, the cells were washed and resuspended in 150 μL PBS. Finally, the thermal infrared imager was used to record the temperature of cells with NIR irradiation at 1 W/cm2 for 5 min. After the cells were seeded for 24 h, 100 µg/mL MC-ss-CDs were incubated with cells, and the NIR laser was used to irradiate the cells for various times. Then, the cells were collected, stained using calcein-AM and PI, and further observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and mitochondrial membrane potential measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake of samples by 4T1 cells was characterized by CLSM and flow cytometry (FCM). Then cells were cultured with sterilized free DOX, MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-ss-CDs (equal to 5 μg/mL DOX). After incubation for 2 h, the 4T1 cells were rinsed with cold PBS. For NIR laser irradiation group, DOX/MC-ss-CDs was illuminated with NIR laser (1 W/cm2) for 5 min and further incubated for 1 h. Thereafter, the 4T1 cells were stained with Hoechst 33258 and visualized using CLSM. Additionally, FCM was also used for assessing cellular uptake. 4T1 cells were cultured in a 6-well plate and incubated with free DOX, MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-ss-CDs (equal to 5 μg/mL DOX). The other process was the same as the CLSM process without the staining process. And FCM (BD FACSCalibur, USA) was applied to measure the mean fluorescence intensity.

After 4T1 cells were cultivated in 24-well plates overnight, PBS, DOX, MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-ss-CDs were added and coincubated with cells with the DOX dose at 10 µg/m for 6 h. For PTT groups, an NIR laser (1.0 W/cm2) was applied to irradiate the cells for 5 min. Then, the cells were stained with DAPI and Rh123 and tested by CLSM to evaluate the damage to the mitochondrial membrane. The quantitative measurement of the mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was also carried out by FCM to determine the Rh123 fluorescence intensity.

4T1 cells were seeded and cultured overnight to evaluate the synergistic effect of thermochemotherapy. A series of concentrations of DOX, MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-ss-CDs were incubated with the cells for 24 h. Subsequently, 2 mg/mL MTT solution (50 μL) was added and incubated for 4 h. For photothermal groups, an 808 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) was used to irradiate the cells for 5 min before the addition of MTT. The combination index (CI) was used to evaluate the synergistic effect of chemotherapy and PTT. CI < 1 indicates synergism, CI = 1 indicates additive effects, and CI > 1 indicates antagonism. 4T1 cell 3D multicellular tumor spheroids (MTSs) were prepared in accordance with reported work [42]. As the diameters of 4T1 MTSs reached approximately 300 µm, the MTSs were incubated with different treatment groups with an equal DOX concentration of 5 µg/mL. The PTT group was illuminated with an 808-nm NIR laser at 1 W/cm2 for 5 min. An optical microscope was used to observe the growth state of the MTSs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and tumor penetration experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy female Balb/c mice (18–22 g) were provided by the Experimental Animal Center of Harbin Medical University. Each mouse was injected subcutaneously with 6 × 106 4T1 cells. After the 4T1 tumors reached 200 mm3 (volume = width2 × length/2), the mice were divided randomly into two groups and were injected intravenously with free DOX and DOX/MC-ss-CDs at a DOX dose of 5 mg/kg. The main organs and tumors were collected at 2, 6 and 12 h after administration. The fluorescence intensity of DOX in tissues was observed by an IVIS Lumina III in vivo imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA).

After the tumor volume of 4T1-bearing mice reached 200 cm3, the MC-ss-CDs were administered intravenously at a dose of 10 mg/kg. Frozen slices of the tumors were collected after 6 h, and the frozen slices were stained with DAPI for CLSM observation.

BALB/c mice (18–20 g) were given subcutaneous injections of 4T1 cells. 4T1 cell-bearing mice were divided into 5 groups: (1) saline group; (2) free DOX group; DOX/MC-ss-CDs; MC-ss-CDs irradiated by NIR laser; DOX/MC-ss-CDs irradiated by NIR laser. A DOX dose of 5 mg/kg was administered to each group. For the thermal therapy groups, the tumors were irradiated at 1 W/cm2 at 6 h post-injection of the formulations on the 0, 3rd and 6th days. During the irradiation time, the temperature of the tumor sites and the images of the mice were recorded. Finally, the tumors and major organs of the mice were removed and fixed with 4% PFA for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. Finally, the biochemical indices of saline and MC-ss-CDs irradiated by the NIR laser group were determined to evaluate the biosecurity.

TEM images (EM–208S, CSIS, USA) was applied to observe the morphology and mesoporous structure of nanoparticles. A nitrogen adsorption analysis analyzer (V-Sorb 2800P, Gold APP Instrument Corporation, China) was adopted to characterize the pore size distribution and surface area. Nano-z90 Nanosizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK) was employed to measure the particle sizes and ξ potentials. Power X-ray diffraction was performed on a Siemens D5005 X-ray diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a UV–vis spectrometer (UNIC, Shanghai, China).

CDs were prepared via a simple hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea in DMF solvent at 190 °C for 8 h. The morphology and size of CDs were observed through TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A and Fig. S1, the CDs displayed a monodispersed spherical shape at approximately 3–4 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs exhibited excitation and emission peak wavelengths at 560 nm and 680 nm, respectively. As shown in the inset image, the prepared CDs showed rose hermosa under daylight and exhibited red fluorescence light under 360 nm UV light irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 1C, the fluorescent emission spectra of CDs exhibited excitation wavelength-independent fluorescent properties with an excitation peak at 560 nm. The above data demonstrated that CDs could emit red fluorescence under different wavelengths.

In addition, the heat-generating capability of CDs was further studied. A NIR laser (808 nm) was employed to irradiate different concentrations of CDs suspensions at 2 W/cm for 5 min. As portrayed in Fig. 1D, the water showed no distinct temperature change under NIR irradiation. In contrast, the temperature of CDs suspensions raised approximately 40 °C at 200 μg/mL, exhibiting a concentration-dependent property. The power-dependent temperature elevation curves of CDs under different power densities are shown in Fig. 1E. Subsequently, the stability of CDs was further studied by repeated NIR irradiation. The temperature elevation capability showed no distinct change after 5 NIR irradiation cycles, as shown in Fig. 1F, proving the distinguished photostability of CDs. Based on the above, the prepared red-emitting CDs had excellent fluorescence properties and good photothermal generating efficiency and photostability, which would lay a solid foundation for their use as NIR therapeutic agents for cancer treatment.

As described in Scheme 1, MC-ss-CDs were prepared by grafting red-emitting CDs onto the mesopores of MC as a controlled switch via cleavable disulfide bonds to achieve multiresponsive drug release and photothermal-enhanced programmed synergetic therapy. The preparation process of MC-COOH was based on previously reported literature [43]. As shown in Fig. 2A, MC-COOH showed a uniform spherical structure and had a mean diameter of 100 nm with ample mesoporous pores; thus, it was applicable for efficient drug loading and stimuli-responsive drug release. The particle size of MC-ss-CDs did not have an obvious difference compared with MC-COOH (Fig. 2B). The TEM image of MC-ss-CDs after incubation with 10 mM GSH is shown in Fig. 2C; many CDs nanoparticles were observed markedly in the surrounding MC carriers. In addition, many methods have been used to confirm the successful grafting of CDs onto the surface of MC-COOH. The isotherms, distribution curves and related parameters of MC-COOH, MC-ss-COOH and MC-ss-CDs are shown in Fig. 2D-E and Table S1. The results indicated that the nitrogen adsorption parameters gradually decreased as the modification proceeded, proving the grafting of CDs onto MC-ss-CDs. The CDs NPs had a similar size to the mesopores of the carrier, indicating that the modified CDs on the openings of MC could block the tunnel to prevent the premature release of drug. The Zeta potentials of each step modification are shown in Fig. 2F. The Zeta potential of MC-ss-CDs was dramatically reduced to − 15.9 mV after the grafting of CDs onto MC-ss-COOH (−28.9 mV). As exhibited in Fig. 2G, the particle size of MC-ss-CDs increased slightly after the modification of CDs.

The human serum albumin (HSA) protein adsorption assay was used to evaluate the effectiveness of grafted CDs on MC-COOH. As shown in Fig. 2H, the HSA adsorption amounts of MC-COOH and MC-ss-COOH were 21.8 % and 15.0 %, respectively. After grafting of CDs, the HSA adsorption percentage was obviously decreased to 3.3 %, indicating that the grafted CDs could prevent HSA from being adsorbed on MC-ss-CDs, thus reducing the clearance rate of nanoparticles in vivo. The hemolysis percentages of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs are shown in Fig. 2I. The hemolysis percentages of MC-COOH were markedly dependent on the concentration. In contrast, the hemolysis percentage of MC-ss-CDs decreased dramatically from 32.3 % for MC-COOH to 1.4 % at 1000 μg/mL, indicating the excellent hemocompatibility of MC-ss-CDs in vivo. This could be illustrated by the fact that the grafting of CDs can obviously reduce the interactions between MC-COOH carriers and erythrocyte membrane proteins on the surface of RBCs.

The photothermal conversion capability of nanoparticles is the key factor in PTT-based thermochemotherapy. Both the MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs showed strong NIR absorption at 808 nm, as shown in Fig. S2. To verify the excellent photothermal conversion effect of the MC-ss-CDs, 808-nm NIR illumination was carried out to evaluate the in vitro photothermal heating characteristics. As displayed in Fig. S3, the temperature of distilled water as a control did not increase obviously for the 5-min irradiation group. In contrast, the MC-COOH suspension exhibited prominent concentration-dependent temperature increase properties. The improved temperature of the MC-COOH suspension at 100 μg/mL increased to 40 °C. Encouragingly, the elevated temperature of MC-ss-CDs exceeded that of MC-COOH under the same conditions, as shown in Fig. 3A, demonstrating that MC-ss-CDs showed a better photothermal generating effect than MC-COOH. Additionally, the photothermal heating features of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs suspensions were also studied under different powers of NIR irradiation, as shown in Figs. S4-S5. The results indicated that the elevated temperature of MC-ss-CDs was superior to that of single mesoporous carbon and single CDs, demonstrating that the opening-gated CDs and MC-COOH played an additional role in photothermal heating [44]. Furthermore, the reusable characteristics and photostability of NIR adsorbing agents were regarded as crucial factors in PTT. The stability of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs under repeated NIR irradiation was studied (Fig. 3B-C). No distinct changes in the generating heating properties of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs were observed after repeated cycles, proving the excellent stability of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs under repeated NIR laser irradiation. To further investigate the photothermal effect of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs, the η values were calculated according to the published literature [41]. The samples (100 μg/mL) were illuminated at 2.0 W/cm2 to calculate the 808 nm laser-triggered heat generation efficiency, as shown in Fig. 3D-E. The η values of MC-COOH and MC-ss-CDs were 24.8 % and 34.8 %, respectively. The enhanced heating generation effects of the MC-ss-CDs carrier were ascribed to the fact that the CDs modification could increase the dispersibility of the system and the good heating generation capacity of CDs. Therefore, MC-ss-CDs showed more potential for PTT-related tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading efficiency and existing state
ce:section.ce:para: The LE% of DOX in DOX/MC-ss-CDs was % owing to the highly orderly mesoporous structure, large surface area, strong adsorption ability of carbon materials and noncovalent interactions [43]. In addition, XRD was applied to measure the physical state of DOX in DOX/MC-ss-CDs. The characteristic crystalline diffraction peaks were found in raw DOX and the physical mixture, as shown in Fig. 3F. However, no obvious crystalline diffraction peaks were observed in DOX/MC-ss-CDs, indicating the non-crystalline state of DOX because of the confined space in the mesopores of MC carriers [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple-stimuli responsive DOX release and dissociation mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH 7.4 PBS was adopted to simulate the blood environment and normal tissues, while pH 5.5 PBS was applied to mimic the endosomes and lysosomes of tumor cells. Fig. 3G displays the drug release profiles of DOX/MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-COOH in release media under different pH conditions. Compared with pH 7.4 PBS, both DOX/MC-ss-CDs and DOX/MC-COOH displayed faster DOX release at pH 5.5 PBS, which was ascribed to the electrostatic interaction between the MC-COOH nanocarriers and DOX becoming weak in an acidic environment, resulting in the accelerated escape of DOX from the mesoporous channels. Nevertheless, in the same pH medium, the release profile of DOX from DOX/MC-COOH was faster than that of DOX/MC-ss-CDs, demonstrating the switching role of CDs in hindering the untimely release behavior of DOX/MC-ss-CDs. In addition, the redox-sensitive release character of DOX/MC-ss-CDs was studied compared with DOX/MC-COOH. As displayed in Fig. 3H, no obvious difference was observed for DOX/MC-COOH in the presence or absence of 10 mM GSH. In contrast, the release of DOX/MC-ss-CDs with 10 mM GSH at pH 5.5 PBS was markedly improved to 37.5 % by contrast with that at pH 5.5 PBS in the absence of GSH (18.7 %), indicating that the redox-sensitive drug release property was due to the disulfide bonds in DOX/MC-ss-CDs. Under the high GSH concentration, the disulfide bonds linking MC-COOH and CDs were cleaved, which made the detachment of CDs from the openings of MC, resulting in boosted DOX release from DOX/MC-ss-CDs.

To confirm the shedding of CDs from the surface of MC nanoparticles under high GSH concentrations, the photothermal effects of the supernatant were evaluated under 10 mM GSH. As described in Fig. S6, the supernatant of MC-ss-CDs in the presence of GSH exhibited an obvious temperature elevation effect (approximately 8 ℃), while the temperature of the upper supernatant of MC-ss-CDs in the absence of GSH did not change markedly. The above experiments showed that the grafted CDs could be detached from MC-ss-CDs due to the breakage of disulfide bonds under a high concentration of GSH.

To study NIR-triggered drug release, DOX/MC-ss-CDs were irradiated under 808 nm NIR at 2.0 W/cm2 for 5 min at 1 h, 4 h and 9 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3I, the DOX release profiles sharply increased under 5 min of NIR irradiation at pH 5.5 PBS, whether in the presence or absence of 10 mM GSH. In addition, the DOX release percentage of DOX/MC-ss-CDs in 10 mM GSH under NIR irradiation was 54.1 %, which was much higher than that in either without GSH (28.8 %) or without NIR irradiation (37.4%) in the same pH 5.5 PBS, indicating the synergistic effect of NIR irradiation and the redox environment. This was because the temperature elevation could boost the molecular thermal motion of the drugs and destroy the mutual force between DOX and the carrier. Because of the foregoing, the DOX/MC-ss-CDs exhibited the pH/redox/NIR synergistic multiple-responsive properties of DOX/MC-ss-CDs.

The excellent photothermal transduction ability of MC-ss-CDs propelled us to further evaluate the cellular photothermal effect. After incubating 4T1 cells with a series of concentrations of MC-COOH or MC-ss-CDs, the cells were illuminated at 1 W/cm2 for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 4D, obvious temperature elevation was observed with increasing MC-ss-CDs concentrations, while blank 4T1 cells did not show an apparent temperature increase under NIR irradiation. It is common knowledge that ablation would occur in tumor cells at a temperature above 42 °C [46]. The increased temperature of 4T1 cells incubated with 50 μg/mL MC-ss-CDs exceeded 10 °C when irradiated for 2 min, indicating dead tumor cells. To further evaluate the effect of MC-ss-CDs as the PTT agent, the calcein-AM/PI cell staining assay was used, as shown in Fig. 4A. The control group showed evident green fluorescence, while the red fluorescence (dead cells) gradually increased as the irradiation time increased. Additionally, all the cells were stained with red fluorescence within 6 min, and the green fluorescence nearly disappeared. Hence, the calcein-AM/PI staining assay results demonstrated that the tumor cells could be fully destroyed under PTT irradiation conditions due to the excellent photothermal effect of the MC-ss-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and cellular drug release
ce:section.ce:para: MC nanocarriers have become novel theranostic nanoplatforms due to their facile functionalization, excellent photothermal properties and supramolecular π-π stacking. Supramolecular π-π stacking can tentatively quench the fluorescence of the loaded or grafted fluorescent molecules attached to MC carriers, and the fluorescence will recover after the molecules detached from the MC vehicles [19,39]. Owing to the fluorescent quenching characteristics of mesoporous carbon-based nanoplatforms, the fluorescence of CDs was momentarily shielded when conjugated onto MC vehicles. To prove the recovery of fluorescence of CDs under high GSH concentrations in the cytoplasm of 4T1 cells, the cellular uptake performances of MC-ss-CDs in the presence or absence of NIR irradiation were analyzed by CLSM. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the relatively weak fluorescence intensity in the cellular nucleus is more likely to be observed owing to the positive charge of CDs detached from MC carriers under the high cytoplasmic GSH concentration. This result implied that MC-ss-CDs could be taken up by 4T1 cells and then triggered the detachment of CDs to accelerate cargo release. Moreover, the recovery of CDs fluorescence is vital for tracking and visualizing drug release. Marked stronger red fluorescence was observed for MC-ss-CDs under NIR irradiation, which was mainly ascribed to the increased cell temperature facilitating the cellular uptake of MC-ss-CDs, followed by the accelerated cleavage of disulfide bonds.

In addition, encouraged by the NIR-triggered release of DOX/MC-ss-CDs, the effects of NIR irradiation on the cellular release of DOX/MC-ss-CDs were further evaluated by CLSM, as shown in Fig. 4C. The red DOX fluorescence and blue fluorescence (nucleus) were colocalized after 2 h of culture of 4T1 cells and free DOX because the small DOX molecule could easily penetrate the cell membrane via passive diffusion to kill tumor cells. No obvious difference in the red fluorescence signal was found with/without NIR irradiation. In comparison, for the DOX/MC-ss-CDs group, red fluorescence from DOX and CDs was observed both in the nucleus and the cytoplasm, indicating that these DOX/MC-ss-CDs nanovesicles could be internalized into the cytoplasm by endocytosis. Furthermore, marked red fluorescence accumulation in the cytoplasm was observed after NIR irradiation. These results demonstrated that NIR irradiation could promote the detachment of CDs from the nanovesicles and facilitate DOX release. Moreover, the cellular release of DOX/MC-ss-CDs was further evaluated by FCM (Fig. 4E). The red fluorescence signal of NIR (+) was nearly 1.8-fold that of NIR (-). The above results verified that the local heating induced by laser irradiation could accelerate cellular CDs detachment and drug release.

The membrane potentials of mitochondria were measured to study the chemo-photothermal mechanism of DOX/MC-ss-CDs. The disruption of the mitochondrial membrane is generally used to evaluate mitochondrial apoptosis. As a fluorescent probe, Rh123 is widely used to detect MMP. When the MMP decreases, the green fluorescence intensity of Rh123 is weakened [47,48]. The detection results of Rh123 by CLSM for different treatment groups are presented in Fig. 5A. In contrast with the control group, the green fluorescence from Rh123 obviously decreased for all the treatment groups, which was ascribed to the chemotherapeutics and the hyperthermia induced by PTT triggering cell apoptosis. As expected, more marked cell apoptosis was observed for the combination of chemotherapy and PTT with the weakest green fluorescence intensity. In addition, the quantitative results of FCM were in accord with the results of CLSM, as shown in Fig. 5B.

The cell viability of DOX/MC-ss-CDs toward 4T1 cells was evaluated by MTT assay. The 4T1 cells incubated with different samples were exposed to the NIR laser to determine the effect of synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy. As displayed in Fig. 5D and E, 4T1 cells incubated with MC-ss-CDs showed a high viability of more than 85 %, proving that MC-ss-CDs were safe and had good biocompatibility. However, for the PTT group, MC-ss-CDs under 1 W/cm2 NIR irradiation showed distinct cytotoxicity up to nearly 50 % at the high MC-ss-CDs concentration. For the free DOX chemotherapy group, a concentration-dependent cell inhibition effect was observed. In contrast with the free DOX group, DOX under NIR irradiation showed similar cell viability at the corresponding concentration, and the IC50 values were almost the same for the two groups ( Table 1), indicating that the NIR laser did not have an obvious inhibitory effect on cell viability. Without laser irradiation, the DOX/MC-ss-CDs group showed slightly higher cell viability than the free DOX group due to incomplete DOX release. Expectedly, compared with chemotherapy or PTT alone, the DOX/MC-ss-CDs group under NIR laser irradiation showed the highest therapeutic effect of chemo-photothermal therapy. The IC50 value of the chemotherapy group was 4.6-fold that of the combination group, as shown in Fig. S7. The combination index (CI) [41] was calculated to be 0.38, proving an efficient synergistic therapeutic effect of chemo-photothermal based on DOX/MC-ss-CDs.

This result was attributed to the increased temperature of 4T1 cells under NIR irradiation promoting the release of DOX, damaging tumor cells by poisonous heat and improving the sensitivity of cells to chemotherapeutic agents to generate maximal cytotoxicity.

To overcome the limitations of the 2D cell model, the therapeutic effect of MC-ss-CDs was further evaluated using 4T1 MTSs. As shown in Fig. 5C, the volume of MTSs in the control group increased gradually. The 4T1 MTSs for the other treatment groups showed various levels of dissociation and collapse. Encouragingly, the 4T1 MTSs in the combined treatment group almost completely fractured after 4 d of treatment for synergistic chemotherapy and PTT. The above data demonstrated that DOX/MC-ss-CDs exhibited good potential as smart nanoplatforms for synergistic chemo-photothermal antitumor treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution study and tumor penetration ability evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: The accumulative number of chemotherapeutic drugs in the tumor tissue is primarily related to antitumor effects. Thus, the distribution and tumor retention capability of DOX/MC-ss-CDs was determined in 4T1-bearing mice compared with free DOX using a fluorescent imager. As presented in Fig. 5G, the fluorescence intensity of the free DOX group reached a maximum at the tumor sites and reduced observably with increasing time. In contrast, after DOX was encapsulated in MC-ss-CDs, the fluorescence signal of DOX reached the peak at 6 h owing to the retention effect and enhanced permeability. The DOX fluorescence signal remained obvious after administration for 12 h. The DOX fluorescence semiquantification results were consistent with the fluorescence pictures shown in Fig. 5H. These results verified that the MC-ss-CDs nanoplatform could realize long-term in vivo circulation, which was beneficial for enhancing the antitumor effect of thermochemotherapy. Inspired by the stimuli-responsive drug release properties and synergistic chemo-photothermal antitumor results of DOX/MC-ss-CDs, the in vivo tumor capacity of MC-ss-CDs was further investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5F, the CDs red fluorescence signals in the superficial tumor were similar to those in the deep-seated tumor tissue, and the fluorescence intensity was relatively uniform throughout the whole region, which implied that MC-ss-CDs could be taken up by tumor tissue and then triggered the detachment of CDs to recover the fluorescence of CDs and visualize the delivery process. These results proved the good tumor penetration ability of the MC-ss-CDs.

Inspired by the in vitro synergistic antitumor effect and improved accumulation in tumor sites, the in vivo antitumor effect of DOX/MC-ss-CDs under NIR irradiation was further investigated. As displayed in Fig. 6H, the IR thermal imaging of tumor sites and temperature variations were recorded through a thermal camera. After continuous irradiation at 1 W/cm2 for 3 min, the temperature of the tumor tissue increased from 28.1° to 51.1 °C for the DOX/MC-ss-CDs + NIR group, and the improved temperature was high enough to accelerate DOX release and ablate the tumor. In contrast, the saline group exhibited a mild temperature change from 27.9° to 36.2 °C. As shown in Fig. 6A-E, the saline group exhibited rapid tumor growth, while the other therapeutic groups showed significant tumor suppression to some extent. For the DOX/MC-ss-CDs group, a moderate tumor inhibition effect was observed due to the inadequate DOX release from DOX/MC-ss-CDs. Although the free DOX group exhibited a more obvious therapeutic effect than the DOX/MC-ss-CDs group, the body weight of the mice decreased significantly after the administration of free DOX (Fig. 6F), proving that free DOX had obvious systemic toxicity. The mouse body weight did not show significant loss for the other groups, indicating the good biosafety of DOX/MC-ss-CDs and MC-ss-CDs. For the single PTT group, the MC-ss-CDs + NIR group showed marked tumor suppression activity. As expected, the combination therapy group (DOX/MC-ss-CDs + NIR) showed the best tumor inhibition effect, and the volume of tumors was much smaller than that before therapy. Similarly, the histogram of relative tumor mass (Fig. 6G) attained after 15 days of treatment also confirmed that the synergistic anticancer effect of DOX/MC-ss-CDs under NIR irradiation was much better than that of any monotherapy.

To further evaluate the potential systemic toxicity on major organs, the heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney were stained with H&E after 15 d of treatment for histological analysis (Fig. 6I). There was obvious cardiotoxicity in the free DOX group. Compared to the saline group, no noticeable changes in morphology and pathological were observed in the major organs except for the free DOX group. The above results illustrated that the DOX/MC-ss-CDs and MC-ss-CDs had excellent antitumor effects and negligible adverse effects. Furthermore, to evaluate the systemic biocompatibility of DOX/MC-ss-CDs, liver functions (alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and aspartate aminotransferase (AST)) and kidney functions (blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CRE)) were investigated. As displayed in Fig. 6J, the DOX/MC-ss-CDs group showed saline-comparable data of biochemical indicators after the whole experimental treatment.

In this work, a redox-responsive size-changeable smart nanoplatform was designed based on MC carriers and CDs with multiresponsive release and combined chemotherapy and PTT to realize programmed tumor therapy. The MC with excellent photothermal transition efficiency was used as the delivery carrier. The CDs were modified on the openings of MC as the switchers to hinder untimely release, the fluorescent agent to track the delivery process and the PTT agent to improve the photothermal effect. The DOX/MC-ss-CDs remained stable in circulation and were promptly divided into DOX/MC nanoparticles and ample CDs-iRGD after internalization by tumor cells. DOX/MC accomplished efficient thermocemotherapy of superficial tumors. The small-sized CDs with improved penetrating ability effectively entered the tumor depth and realized photothermal ablation and fluorescence imaging. DOX/MC-ss-CDs showed a higher η of 34.8% compared with MC and an outstanding LE% of 35.4% for DOX. The release results indicated that DOX/MC-ss-CDs possessed pH/redox/NIR multiresponsive release properties. The cellular uptake assay demonstrated accelerated DOX release under NIR irradiation and the fluorescence imaging ability of CDs. Moreover, DOX/MC-ss-CDs exhibited prominent synergistic chemophotothermal effects with a CI of 0.38 at the cytological level and in vivo antitumor experiments at low-power irradiation. The impressive advancements in this study were that the drug release visualization and penetration ability were combined in the system. Meanwhile, marked photothermal conversion efficiency was designed for programmed tumor therapy. This present drug delivery platform employs a multimode therapeutic strategy to destroy cancer cells at different depths, thus providing a good reference for synergistic cancer treatment.
